text: This video demonstrates robot assisted surgery for bilateral pheochromocytoma in a fifteen year old girl. A fifteen year old girl presented with hypertension in the last two years. Currently her blood pressure was one ninety by hundred and ten millimeters of mercury. She complained of palpitations and sweating episodes. Her body mass index was twenty six and she gives a significant family history of younger brother who underwent surgery for left pheochromocytoma laparoscopically a month back by our team. A CT scan revealed bilateral adrenal masses, the right one measuring approximately four and a half centimeters abutting the liver and the left one measuring approximately two point five centimeter directly relating to the left renal vein. There was an accessory inferior hepatic vein which was traveling very close to the adrenal mass.

Considering that this was a bilateral procedure, we had to plan the ports in such a way that most of the ports could be utilized for both sides and one extra working port was taken on each side. Talking of the robot was over the ipsilateral shoulder with the patient in lateral position. There are several challenges while preparing patients for pheochromocytoma surgery. We took a couple of weeks to stabilize the patient's blood pressure using alpha blockade followed by beta blockade and adequate fluid loading. There were some challenges during the surgery with the fact that this was bilateral. There could be abandonment of the procedure after one side. There is always a risk of hemodynamic instability during the procedure. Proximity to major vascular structures makes it more challenging.

The surgeon begins the procedure on the right side by dissecting the tumor from the undersurface of the liver up to the diaphragm. The robotic instruments' endo-wrist movements aid in creating a plane between the inferior vena cava and the tumor. The adrenal vein is identified, dissected, clipped with hemolog clips, and divided. The surgeon carefully dissects the tumor's undersurface and from the upper pole of the kidney, clipping and dividing major vascular attachments to prevent bleeding. Clips and bipolar energy are used to manage the vascular nature of the pheochromocytoma, while preserving as much normal adrenal tissue as possible.

The surgeon preserves as much normal adrenal tissue as possible. The excised specimen is packed and placed under the left lobe of the liver. Hemostasis is achieved using Surgicel for low-pressure, low-volume bleeding.

The robotic instruments are repositioned for surgery on the left side. The surgeon carefully dissects surrounding structures, including the spleen, tail of the pancreas, upper pole of the kidney, and colon, away from the left adrenal gland. The adrenal vein is looped and controlled to preserve it during the adrenal-sparing procedure. The surgeon performs enucleoresection of the tumor from the lateral limb of the left adrenal gland using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection, with hemolog clips applied as needed. Some bleeding is observed during the dissection process.

The preserved left adrenal gland is visible, with hemostasis achieved and a drain placed. The specimen, including the right-sided adrenal gland with the tumor and the left-sided adrenal tumor, is packed and retrieved. The video shows the specimen, confirming bilateral pheochromocytoma.

She was discharged on post- drain removal. Blood pressure at the time of discharge was one ten by seventy millimeters of mercury with a pulse rate of eighty per minute. Since this is a familial form of pheochromocytoma, genetic testing has been done. The plan is to keep on long-term follow-up for any possible recurrence or associated tumors. In conclusion, bilateral procedure for Pheo in the same sitting is possible provided the patient is optimally prepared and remains hemodynamically stable. Preoperative preparation is very important. Listed movements of the robotic instrument makes accurate movements possible during surgery and adrenal sparing surgery is necessary for bilateral cases and familial syndromes to prevent adrenal insufficiency. Thank you.
